title: Practice Midterm Q1 — Asymptotic T/F (6 pts)
statement:
For each claim, state true or false. No proofs or justifications needed. Functions $f, g$ are from $\mathbb{N}$ to $\mathbb{N}$.

1. If $f(n) = \Theta(n^3)$ and $g(n) = O(n^2)$, then $f(n)\cdot g(n) = \Theta(n^5)$.
2. $\sqrt{\log_2 n} = \Theta(\log_2 \sqrt{n})$.
3. $T(n) = T(n/2) + n,\ T(1) = 1 \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.
4. $T(n) = T(n-1) + n,\ T(1) = 1 \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.
5. Merge Sort runs in $O(n \log n)$ on average but not in the absolute worst case.
6. $n$ distinct numbers split into groups of $9$; let $M$ be the median of the group medians. Then at most $13n/18$ of the original numbers could be greater than $M$.

steps.title: 1. $f \cdot g = \Theta(n^5)$?
steps.answer: **False.** $f \cdot g = O(n^5)$ holds, but $\Theta$ requires a matching lower bound.
steps.body: We have $f = \Theta(n^3)$ meaning $f(n)$ is both $O(n^3)$ and $\Omega(n^3)$. But $g = O(n^2)$ only gives an *upper* bound on $g$; $g$ could be as small as a constant (e.g. $g(n) = 1$, which is indeed $O(n^2)$). Then $f \cdot g$ would be $\Theta(n^3)$, not $\Theta(n^5)$. So no $\Omega(n^5)$ bound is forced.

steps.title: 2. $\sqrt{\log_2 n} = \Theta(\log_2 \sqrt{n})$?
steps.answer: **False.** The RHS equals $\tfrac{1}{2}\log_2 n$, which is $\Theta(\log n)$ — way bigger than $\sqrt{\log n}$.
steps.body: By the log power rule, $\log_2 \sqrt{n} = \log_2 n^{1/2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \log_2 n$. So the RHS is $\Theta(\log n)$. The LHS is $\sqrt{\log n} = (\log n)^{1/2}$, which grows much slower. Since $\log n$ beats $\sqrt{\log n}$ (the ratio $\log n / \sqrt{\log n} = \sqrt{\log n} \to \infty$), we have $\sqrt{\log n} \ne \Omega(\log n)$, so they're not $\Theta$.

tips.label: Log power rule
tips.content: $\log(x^k) = k \log x$, so $\log \sqrt{n} = \log n^{1/2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \log n$. This is the single step that breaks this whole claim.

steps.title: 3. $T(n) = T(n/2) + n = \Theta(n^2)$?
steps.body:
Unroll the recurrence:

$T(n) = n + T(n/2) = n + n/2 + T(n/4) = \cdots = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \frac{n}{2^i} + T(1)$.

The sum $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^i} = 2$, so the whole sum is $\le 2n$. Hence $T(n) = O(n)$. The first term $n$ gives $T(n) = \Omega(n)$. So $T(n) = \Theta(n)$.

tips.label: Infinite geometric
tips.content: $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} r^i = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ for $0 \le r < 1$. Here $r = 1/2$ so the sum is $2$. This is the standard way to upper-bound a decreasing geometric sum from a recursion tree.

tips.label: Tree-view check
tips.content: Level $i$: $1$ subproblem of size $n/2^i$, non-recursive work $n/2^i$. Level work is decreasing geometric → dominated by the root. $\Theta(n)$.

steps.title: 4. $T(n) = T(n-1) + n = \Theta(n^2)$?
steps.body:
Unroll: $T(n) = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + \cdots + 2 + T(1) = T(1) + \sum_{k=2}^{n} k$.

The sum $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$. So $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. This is the same recurrence as the worst-case Quick Sort.

tips.label: Arithmetic series
tips.content: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$. Memorize this. Comes up in every Quick-Sort-worst-case argument.

steps.title: 5. Merge Sort: $O(n\log n)$ average but not worst case?
steps.answer: **False.** Merge Sort is $\Theta(n \log n)$ in the **worst** case. No dependence on input distribution.
steps.body: Merge Sort's recurrence is $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ regardless of input. It always does the same split and merge. The confusion here is with **Quick Sort** — whose worst case really is $\Theta(n^2)$ while the average is $\Theta(n \log n)$.

steps.title: 6. Groups of 9, median of medians $M$: at most $13n/18$ greater than $M$?
steps.body:
Generalize the median-of-medians guarantee. With groups of $k = 9$: there are $n/9$ groups. The median-of-medians $M$ is the median of these $n/9$ values, so at least $n/18$ group medians are $\le M$.

Consider any group whose median $m \le M$. Sort the group: it looks like $(a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4, m, a_6, a_7, a_8, a_9)$. Here $a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4, m$ — **five** elements — are all $\le m \le M$.

So each such group guarantees 5 elements $\le M$. Total: $\frac{n}{18} \cdot 5 = \frac{5n}{18}$.

Hence at most $n - \frac{5n}{18} = \frac{13n}{18}$ elements can be strictly greater than $M$. ✓

tips.label: General group-size formula
tips.content: For groups of size $k$ (odd): $\lceil k/2 \rceil$ elements are $\le m$ in each group where $m \le M$. Half of the $n/k$ group medians are $\le M$. So at least $(n/(2k)) \cdot \lceil k/2\rceil$ elements are guaranteed $\le M$.

tips.label: Group of 5
tips.content: $(n/10) \cdot 3 = 3n/10$ elements $\le M$, so at most $7n/10$ greater. This is the standard median-of-medians bound.

tips.label: Group of 9
tips.content: $(n/18) \cdot 5 = 5n/18$ elements $\le M$, so at most $13n/18$ greater. ✓

answerKey: 1. False  2. False  3. False  4. True  5. False  6. True
